text:
Aerobic granular sludge (AGS) is commonly used in wastewater treatment plants to deal with a complex composition of soluble organic pollutants. Stable AGS formation generally requires anaerobic hydrolysis of the readily biodegradable Volatile Fatty Acids (VFA), particularly acetate, into the polymers that serve as internal storage materials. If AGS growth proceeds on the readily available dissolved COD under aerobiosis, it leads to unstable, highly filamentous AGS. Therefore, it is very important to understand how different carbon sources affect the stability and composition of AGS at the initial stage of its development. This study investigated the effects of acetate, as well as several other organic carbon sources namely methanol, n-butanol, n-propanol, propionaldehyde and valeraldehyde at 35 °C on AGS formation.

The developed granular sludge culture exhibited a stable microbial population mainly consisting of the methanogenic archaeon *Methanomethylovorans uponensis* (MMur) adapted to high concentrations of methanol. In the anaerobic feeding phase, MMur assimilated almost all the supplied methanol through the formation of methane and carbon dioxide in a relatively short period of 1 h. Simultaneously, it was able to resist and utilize the resulting high dissolved oxygen concentration (up to 3.5 mg O₂ L⁻¹) throughout the several subsequent aeration periods. Meanwhile, propionaldehyde and valeraldehyde were subject to anaerobic disproportionation into their corresponding alcohols and carboxylic acids. However, the formed organic acids were accumulated in the polymer form while the absorbed alcohols—both the original and the conversion products—were degraded under the aerobic conditions.

The substrates that were not consumed for the production of storage polymers might be the contributing factors to granule instability, and stable Aerobic Granular Sludge (AGS) could still be formed through adequate incorporation of the non-consumed organics into the sludge structure and the slower consumption by microorganisms during the aeration phase. The metabolic strategies and the feasibility of managing various industrial complex substrates by AGS have been revealed in this study.
